subject: Rate articles by signal (interest + helpful), with filter and sort
body:
Add a per-article editorial signal — how interesting (novelty/insight)
and how helpful (practical/reference value) each piece is, 1-5 each —
living in each article's own frontmatter, and derive a 1-5 signal level
(Niche -> Solid -> Notable -> High -> Essential) from the two.

- Schema: optional `interest`/`helpful` on articleFrontmatter (Zod 1-5),
  plus an `articleSignal()` derivation helper. Rated all 79 articles.
- Articles list: a "High signal" filter alongside All/Featured, a sort
  row (newest / signal / interesting / helpful), and a signal-bar badge
  on every card — all persisted to the URL (back-compat with ?featured=1).
- /api/articles now exposes the derived signal so agents can sort/filter
  the same way the UI does.

subject: Add Gigatoken article
body:
Flagship article on Gigatoken (Marcel Rod), a drop-in tokenizer ~1000x
faster than HuggingFace's — GB/s throughput — by rewriting the regex
pretokenization step with SIMD and caching pretoken->token mappings,
not by out-threading (HF and tiktoken already run multithreaded Rust).

- PretokenPipeline interactive: naive vs gigatoken time breakdown showing
  regex pretokenization as the bottleneck, plus the SIMD + cache moves.
- ThroughputBars interactive: per-CPU (EPYC / M4 Max / Ryzen) throughput
  and speedup, colored by tokenizer family — with the honest BPE (~1000x)
  vs SentencePiece (~10-20x) split baked in.
- CommonCrawlClock interactive: time to tokenize a dataset, gigatoken vs
  HuggingFace, anchored on the README's "all of Common Crawl (130T tokens)
  in ~6.5 hours" claim.
- Real throughput figure (converted from the repo's SVG onto white).
- Notes the documented edges (SentencePiece under-optimized, no WordPiece,
  Windows untested, ABI3 overhead) and the AI-use disclosure.

subject: Agent: GPT-5.4 model router + fallback, and a rate limiter
body:
Replace the single-model agent config with a small router over the OpenAI
GPT-5.4 family and add a mini WAF, per request.

Model router + fallback (lib/chat.ts):
- Tiers nano / mini / main map to gpt-5.4-nano / gpt-5.4-mini / gpt-5.4
  (overridable via CHAT_MODEL_NANO / CHAT_MODEL / CHAT_MODEL_MAIN).
- routeTier() is a cheap heuristic — short factual lookups -> nano,
  comparative/explanatory/long or multi-part questions -> main, else
  mini. No extra LLM round-trip.
- generateWithFallback() (/api/ask, MCP) and streamWithFallback()
  (/api/chat) walk the ladder: if a model errors they drop to the next,
  so one bad/unavailable model never takes the agent down. Streaming only
  falls back before the first token is emitted. The response reports the
  model that actually answered.

Rate limiter / mini WAF (lib/rate-limit.ts):
- In-memory two-window limiter (burst + sustained) keyed by client IP,
  applied to /api/chat (5/10s, 20/min) and /api/ask (8/10s, 30/min).
  Returns 429 with retry-after + x-ratelimit-* headers. Dependency-free;
  best-effort per-instance (documented).

Also: stop leaking the OPENAI_API_KEY env-var name in the offline notices
(generic "chat is offline" copy) and refresh the deploy-runbook env table.

subject: Add stylometric similarity heatmap to /models
body:
A "which models write alike" heat map: each model answered eight fixed
prompts, and cells are the symmetric KL divergence between their
character-trigram distributions — style from the words alone. Same-lab
families cluster (Anthropic's Opus/Sonnet/Fable, the Gemini line); the
telling cells are the low-divergence ones across labs (GLM 5.2 sits
closest to Gemini 3.1 Pro at 0.418 bits), and GPT 5.4 Mini reads as the
stylistic outlier.

Data and method reproduced faithfully from Typebulb's "You're relatively
right!" bulb (char trigrams, lambda=0.8 background interpolation,
cross-entropy to KL in bits, Jeffreys-symmetrised), baked into a
generated data/model-stylometry.ts (22 models, 7 labs) with the source
attributed on the page and in the component. The heatmap component is
house-style: Typebulb's blue-to-grey ramp, hover/click to inspect a pair,
a ramp legend and a detail panel.

subject: Add LongStraw article: million-token GRPO on a fixed GPU budget
body:
Flagship article on LongStraw (arXiv 2607.14952, MindLab + Fudan) — a
systems-feasibility result for long-context RL post-training. Frames it
honestly throughout as a "the step executes" claim, not an accuracy one:
the paper is careful to say it measures response-only execution, not
full-sequence gradient parity, and the article foregrounds that.

Content: resident state (detached stopgrad prompt capture) + response
replay (rebuild one GRPO response graph at a time) so group size G
becomes a schedule, not a memory multiplier; the two-architecture
instantiation (Qwen3.6-27B on 8 H20 with GDN/CP8, GLM-5.2 on 32 H20 with
MLA/DSA/MoE over CP32/EP32); the measured numbers (2,097,152 positions at
G=2/G=8 with +0.208 GB, 4,456,448 positions with 8-step prefix reuse at
83.894 GB/rank under the 150.755 GB ceiling); and the stale-prefix
refresh knob.

- GroupSerialization interactive: drag G, watch the measured serial peak
  stay flat vs an illustrative "hold all graphs" bar that OOMs.
- PrefixReuseDrift interactive: the paper's 1M fresh-prefix oracle at
  steps 1/2/4/8, with a reuse-vs-recapture verdict.
- Two real figures (graph-boundary method diagram, memory-vs-positions
  results plot), cropped from the PDF, flattened onto white, served
  locally.
- BenchBars for the context-reached leap (256K to 2M-4.45M positions).
- A warning Callout enumerating exactly what the paper does NOT claim.

Cross-links glm-5-2 (one of the two target models) and
frontier-rl-cheaper (the sibling systems-first RL take).

subject: Add article: the harness as compositional generalizer
body:
Flagship article on Alex Zhang & Omar Khattab's "Language model harnesses
are compositional generalizers" — the argument that the inductive bias
for compositional generalization can live in the harness, not the weights.
An RLM keeps every LM call locally in-distribution (context offloading +
programmatic sub-calls), so it can be RL-trained on short tasks and
generalize to ones 8-32x longer, and across domains, while the base
Transformer flatlines.

- LidBand interactive: drag the eval length multiplier; watch the base
  Transformer's single context go out-of-distribution (context rot) while
  the RLM's per-call slices stay in-distribution, with diverging eval
  rewards and the eval-lift readout.
- ContextOffload interactive: append (ReAct/CodeAct) vs offload (RLM);
  drag task steps and watch the root context cross the context-rot line
  vs staying a short task-agnostic prefix.
- Three real figures (LID principle, the two mechanisms, six-benchmark
  length-generalization curves), flattened onto white and served locally.
- BenchBars for how much longer each benchmark's eval tasks run.

Distinct from the existing agent-harness (loop engineering) and
harness-effect (token economics) articles; cross-links both.

subject: Add the Motif 2.6B article
body:
content/articles/motif-2-6b.mdx (2026-07-20) — a write-up of Motif Technologies'
Motif 2.6B (arXiv 2508.09148): a small dense model that ships two rarely-seen
architecture bets at scale — Differential Attention and PolyNorm, a learned
degree-3 activation — trained on 2.5T tokens with a data-mixing scheduler that
swings the corpus from broad web text into math/code/reasoning, WSD + a simple
moving average over the last six checkpoints, and a heavily-synthesized SFT +
two-stage DPO. The payoff: a 2.6B model matching 7-8B models on HumanEval/MATH.

- components/articles/motif-2-6b/diff-attention.tsx — the two-softmax subtraction
  with a λ slider; watch attention on filler tokens drain as the noise map is
  subtracted out.
- components/articles/motif-2-6b/poly-norm.tsx — the degree-3 learned activation
  plotted against a GELU reference, with a1/a2/a3 weight sliders.
- components/articles/motif-2-6b/data-schedule.tsx — the data-mixing scheduler:
  drag training progress and watch the eight-group corpus mix morph from the
  start mix to the end mix (Table 2's real initial→final ratios).
- BenchBars for the honest scoreboard (HumanEval, MATH, MMLU vs Mistral 7B /
  Gemma 2 2B / Llama 3 8B).
- public/articles/motif-2-6b/fig1-2.png — the paper's architecture and

the 273k-token corpus dump it replaced, the three-tool design (BM25 search,

subject: Rebuild the site agent: retrieve on demand instead of dumping the corpus
body:
buildSystemPrompt() pasted the ENTIRE content corpus into every request —
~1.09M chars / ~273k tokens, past the serving model's input window, exactly the
failure the code's own "switch to retrieval at ~100K" comment warned about.

Replace it with a small tool-using agent (Vercel AI SDK):
- lib/chat.ts — the system prompt now carries only a compact catalog (kind/slug +
  title + one-line description, ~5k tokens). Three tools do the rest:
  search_content (BM25 over contextualized chunks, from lib/search), get_content
  (fetch one page's markdown on demand), and think (a no-op reasoning scratchpad,
  per Anthropic's think-tool). The loop is bounded by stopWhen: stepCountIs(6).
- Deliberately three tools, declared upfront (cf. the pi coding agent's "four
  tools are all you need"); loading context — not tool schemas — on demand is the
  Kimi dynamic-loading idea applied where our payload actually is.
- Wire the same tool set into all three surfaces: /api/chat (streaming),
  /api/ask (one-shot), and the ask_satyajit MCP tool — one harness, not three.
- Upgrade the MCP search_content tool from naive substring to the same BM25
  engine; drop the now-dead substring path.

Per request: ~273k tokens every time -> ~5k catalog + the 1-3 pages actually
fetched. typecheck green; tool execute() paths verified directly (search ranks,
get_content returns page + data-page markdown, think is a clean no-op). The live
loop needs GOOGLE_API_KEY (set in prod).

subject: Add three diagrams to the BM25 article
body:
The article covered k1 (saturation) and b (length) with interactive diagrams but
explained the IDF term and the inverted index only in prose. Fill both gaps and
add a hero that maps the whole formula.

- components/articles/bm25/formula-anatomy.tsx — a zero-JS hero decomposing the
  equation into three color-coded factors (rarity/IDF, saturation/k1, length/b),
  each mapped to the job it does. The article's map, up front.
- components/articles/bm25/idf-rarity.tsx — the missing IDF diagram: a log-scaled
  IDF(n) curve with real example terms placed from a rare error code to the
  ubiquitous "the", and a draggable document-frequency marker. Shows why rarity is
  almost the whole score and why BM25 needs no stopword list.
- components/articles/bm25/inverted-index.tsx — the mechanism the piece only
  described: term -> postings lists, a query lighting only its own postings, and a
  "scored 4 of 6 · 2 never touched" counter over the same 6-doc corpus as the
  live scorer.

subject: Rebuild corpus search as BM25 over contextualized chunks
body:
lib/search.ts was a substring indexOf: first match wins, no ranking, and any
multi-word query that isn't a verbatim phrase returned nothing. Replace it with
the two techniques the site just wrote up — BM25 (/articles/bm25) for ranking,
and Contextual Retrieval (/blog/contextual-retrieval) for the chunks.

- Chunk each document on headings, packed to a ~90-word budget, MDX/markdown
  stripped to prose.
- Contextualize every chunk deterministically before indexing: prepend the
  document's title, description, tags, and nearest heading (weighted 0.5 so the
  shared context terms don't dominate the passage's own words). No Claude call —
  the context a chunk lost is already in its frontmatter and headings.
- Score with Lucene's exact BM25 (non-negative IDF, k1=1.2, b=0.75), matching
  the /articles/bm25 walkthrough. A term->chunks postings map means a query only
  touches chunks that share a term.
- Collapse chunk scores to their parent document; the best chunk becomes the
  snippet and its heading becomes the result `field`, so /api/search tells an
  agent which section matched. Results now carry a `score`.

Backs both /api/search (JSON for agents) and the /search page unchanged.

subject: Inkling: call out the architecture surprises (embedding RMSNorm, kernel-4 convs, relative bias)
body:
Confirmed from the HF config.json and added the details that make Inkling's
backbone distinctive:
- separate RMSNorm on the token embeddings before the per-block norms
  (use_embed_norm=true) — the easy-to-miss one
- short depthwise convs (kernel size 4, use_sconv) in several places: after
  K/V projections and on residual branches
- relative position bias (d_rel=16, rel_extent=1024), not RoPE
Also corrected the attention detail to 64 Q / 8 KV heads, 6144 dim, and the
55 local / 11 global layer split (the real 5:1 ratio).

subject: Add GRAPE explainer (arXiv 2512.07805): position encodings as one group action
body:
GRAPE (Group Representational Position Encoding; Princeton/UCLA/Tsinghua,
ICLR 2026) unifies positional encodings as a single group action
G(n)=exp(nωL) obeying the exact relative law G(t-s)=G(s)^-1 G(t); the
generator type picks the family. Multiplicative (rank-2 skew L in so(d)) is
norm-preserving rotation via Rodrigues and recovers RoPE exactly; additive
(rank-1 nilpotent lift in GL(d+k)) recovers ALiBi exactly, and FoX's
forget-gate bias is exactly Path-Integral GRAPE-AP.

Results (353M/770M, FineWeb-Edu 50B, 0-shot NLU avg): GRAPE-AP best (353M
53.25 vs RoPE 51.73 / ALiBi 52.87 / FoX 52.96; 770M 56.91 vs 55.76 / 56.44
/ 56.30). Three interactives: generator-action (rotation vs shear + relative
score), family-map (taxonomy of RoPE/ALiBi/FoX as special cases),
additive-bias (ALiBi fan vs content-gated slope). Two BenchBars + 2 real
figures (from the PDF).

Honest: gains are small (~0.3-1.5 pts) at small scale; multiplicative GRAPE-M
underperforms RoPE at 770M; no long-context/extrapolation tests despite the
motivation; no wall-clock/FLOP; author-reimplemented baselines.

subject: Add LOTUS explainer (arXiv 2606.31779): closing the latent-vs-explicit reasoning gap with looped Transformers
body:
LOTUS (Fan, Svete, Lee 2026) is the first latent chain-of-thought method to
close the gap to explicit CoT at 3B while cutting thought-phase latency
2.5x-6.9x. A looped padded Transformer refines a fixed latent region (K
blocks x c tokens) in parallel over R passes of the same weights, so it
costs R forward passes rather than one-per-token. Trained with a combined
answer + position-aligned step loss through the base LM head.

Results (Llama-3B/GSM8K): LOTUS 70.0 vs explicit 71.5 in-domain, ahead OOD
(63.9 vs 62.1); thought latency 133 vs 339 ms (2.5x), 141 vs 964 ms (6.9x)
on natural-language CoT at ~equal accuracy. Ablations: both losses needed
(gold recovery 9% either alone -> 71% both); infer-time depth peaks at the
trained R=6.

Three interactives: looped-forward (the R-pass latent refinement),
thought-latency (LOTUS finishing while explicit CoT still decodes),
loop-depth (accuracy vs R, train/infer curves). Two BenchBars + 2 real
figures. Honest: math-only eval; K,c,R are fixed budgets set in advance,
chains longer than K steps fall back to autoregressive. Cross-links
how-llm-inference-works, multi-token-prediction, illada, jacobian-lens.

subject: Add Inkling explainer: Thinking Machines' 975B/41B open-weights multimodal MoE
body:
Inkling (Thinking Machines Lab): open-weights MoE, 975B total / 41B active,
1M context, encoder-free multimodal. Detailed walkthrough + 3 interactives +
BenchBars. No static figures exist (announcement charts are client-rendered
inline SVG) so the interactives carry it.

Architecture: 256 routed + 2 shared experts (6 active/token), sigmoid router
w/ aux-loss-free balancing; interleaved sliding-window:global attention 5:1,
8 KV heads; relative positional embeddings (not RoPE); short convs after K/V.
Encoder-free multimodal: audio as dMel, images as 40x40 patches via 4-layer
hMLP. Trained 45T tokens, Muon+Adam, GB300; NVFP4 weights released.

Signature ideas: controllable effort (Nemotron-3-Ultra-equivalent Terminal
Bench at ~1/3 the tokens via system message + per-token cost); RL reasoning
compression (CoT shortened emergently); log-linear reward 0.264->0.356 over
30M+ rollouts. Interactives: effort-curve, architecture-stack, rl-scaling.

Honesty: the lab itself says it's 'not the strongest overall model' — a
customizable open-weights base, not SOTA; benchmarks vendor-reported on a
provider-selected set; the 1/3-token / log-linear / emergent-compression
claims are their own unaudited measurements. Cross-links MoE, nemotron-nvfp4,
minimax/mimo (SWA), ring-zero (RL).

subject: arXiv digest 2026-07-13: 6 papers (video-diffusion seriality gap, dLLM acceleration, diffusion ASR, agent-eval co-evolution, inhibited attention, LiDAR+VFM)

against prior digests. Standouts: The Seriality Gap in Video Diffusion Models

subject: Add featured Ring-Zero explainer (arXiv 2607.12395): zero RL at a trillion parameters
body:
Special/flagship, starred (featured: true). Ring-Zero (Ling/Ring team)
runs zero RL (verifiable reward, no SFT) on a 1T-param / 63B-active MoE,
delivered as a stable four-stage pipeline.

Detailed walkthrough with the real math: token-level clipped-importance
objective; the training-inference ratio correction (importance ratio
rho = pi^Megatron / pi^SGLang, clipped at eps_high=5, no lower bound) that
keeps training stable indefinitely where naive collapses ~800 steps; the
token->sample-level switch (1/|o_i|); self-distillation reset. Headline:
full pipeline hits AIME24/25/26 = 94.1/92.3/93.2; discovery (pass@1024
saturates ~step 800) vs sharpening (pass@1 keeps rising).

Four interactives: pipeline-stages, ratio-correction (naive vs corrected
vs the clip), discovery-sharpening (real pass@1/pass@1024 curves),
adaptive-depth (tier budgets + negative-transfer caveat). Three BenchBars
+ 3 real paper figures.

Honesty: trails frontier (93.2 vs 95.7-98.3 on AIME26); RL sharpens within
the pretraining boundary, doesn't transcend it; ablations are on the 104B
flash model not the 1T; 64k context cap; weights/infra unreleased;
emergent-behavior claims are qualitative; competitor numbers author-reported.

subject: Add Bonsai 27B explainer: end-to-end ternary/1-bit Qwen3.6-27B that fits on a phone
body:
PrismML's Bonsai 27B quantizes a full-precision Qwen3.6-27B end to end
(embeddings, attention, MLPs, LM head + a 4-bit vision tower) into two
variants: ternary ({-1,0,+1} + FP16 group scale, 1.71 bits, 5.9GB) and
1-bit ({-1,+1} + scale, 1.125 bits, 3.9GB) — collapsing 54GB so it fits a
phone's app-memory budget. Apache 2.0; capability is Qwen's.

Three interactives: footprint (bit-encoding + 54->3.9GB vs ~6GB phone
line), precision-map (end-to-end low-bit vs typical partial PTQ that keeps
embeddings/attention/head at FP16), retention (per-category score across
FP16/ternary/1-bit with a 1-bit-retention strip). One BenchBars.

Honesty: capability is Qwen3.6-27B's (compression, not a new model);
vendor-reported on PrismML's own 15-benchmark suite in thinking mode; the
~90/95% retention headline masks uneven drops — math holds ~96% at 1-bit
while agentic tool-calling (80->66) and vision (72.6->59.6) lose ~18%;
'intelligence density'/'first 27B on a phone'/'10x' are marketing; speed is
hardware-specific. No whitepaper figures (repo needs access) — interactives
carry it. Cross-links nemotron-nvfp4, turboquant, how-llm-inference-works.

subject: Add Soofi S explainer: a sovereign German/English hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE
body:
Soofi S 30B-A3B (the Soofi-Team, a German consortium; funded by the BMWE,
trained on Deutsche Telekom's Munich Industrial AI Cloud). Built to the
diagram bar with real report figures + three interactives + honest caveats.

Architecture (reuses Nemotron-3-Nano): 52 layers = 23 Mamba-2 + 23 granular
MoE + 6 GQA; 31.6B total / ~3.2B active. Only 6 of 52 layers keep a KV cache
(~6 KB/token, 11-53x below dense), the 23 Mamba-2 layers hold a fixed-size
recurrent state — so decode TPS stays near-flat 4K->256K (9.2x Ministral 14B
at 40K; TTFT at 256K 373s vs 6,429s for dense Qwen3 32B). ~27T tokens,
three-phase WSD, German up-weighted to 15% (>3x reference).

Interactives: throughput-scaling (flat vs decaying baselines), hybrid-stack
(52-layer Mamba/MoE/GQA, KV-cache-layer highlight), moe-routing (128+2
experts, 6 activated). Two BenchBars (German aggregate; active-params).

Honesty: consortium report, not peer-reviewed; author-reported numbers,
author-defined Capability Index, author-selected baselines/protocol; 3B
active (not 30B dense); 'best among fully open' is scoped, and vs open-WEIGHT
models Qwen3.5 35B-A3B still leads — the cleanest result is +4.2 DE over the
architecture-identical Nemotron 3 Nano. Figures served locally.

subject: Add Mach-Mind-4-Flash explainer (arXiv 2607.09375): 100B-class scores from a 35B/3B-active MoE, by post-training alone
body:
Li Auto's Mach-Mind-4-Flash pushes a compact Qwen3.5-35B-A3B base to
100B-class results with post-training only ('specialize, then integrate').
Built to the diagram bar with real paper figures + two mechanism
interactives + honest caveats.

Mechanisms:
- MOPD (Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation): route each sample to one
  frozen domain teacher, token-level reverse-KL on the student's own
  rollouts — kills the see-saw degradation of mixed-reward RL.
- HMPO: length budget = median length of correct rollouts (G=10), cosine
  token reward gated behind correctness; 19-46% fewer tokens for <=0.7pp
  accuracy loss.

Interactives: mopd-fusion (teachers -> student, MOPD vs mixed-reward
see-saw), hmpo-budget (group rollouts + cosine reward + median budget,
live R_final). Two BenchBars (AIME'26 across scales; the five benchmarks
where the 3B-active model leads both 122B and 1T models).

Honest: single-vendor self-reported, not a SOTA claim (trails 309B MiMo-V2
and 1T Kimi-K2.5 on AIME'26); MOPD leaves a gap on repo-level SWE; HMPO is
single-turn; agent-track fusion is mixed. Figures served locally.

subject: Fix MDX build break + add an MDX-compile validation gate
body:
The Vercel build failed on kat-coder-agentic-training.mdx: a bare '<2%'
in prose — MDX reads '<' as the start of a JSX tag. Escaped it to
'<2%'.

Root cause: 'pnpm validate' only checked frontmatter (validate:content),
never compiled the MDX body, so JSX/MDX syntax errors slipped through to
the build. Added scripts/validate-mdx.mts which compiles every content
MDX file with the same remark plugins as next.config.ts (frontmatter +
gfm + math, so '$…$' comparisons stay safe) and fails loudly with the
file:line and a fix tip. Wired into 'pnpm validate' as validate:mdx.
Verified it reproduces the exact build error on a planted bad file and
passes on the real tree (77 files). Documented the escape rule and the
new gate in CLAUDE.md.

subject: Add three paper explainers: Switch Transformer, KAT-Coder, MusaCoder
body:
- switch-transformer (arXiv 2101.03961): the top-1-routing paper the
  modern MoE zoo descends from. Capacity factor + token dropping, load-
  balancing loss, selective precision, 1.6T sparse scaling, ~7x sample-
  efficiency. Interactive top-1<->top-2 routing toggle + capacity/drop
  visualizer; real Fig 2 (routing) + Fig 5 (7x curve). Honest: 1.6T is
  sparse, 7x is matched-FLOPs sample-efficiency bought with memory/comms,
  top-1 not strictly better than top-2, dropped tokens are lost info.
- kat-coder-agentic-training (arXiv 2607.05471, KAT-Coder-V2.5,
  Kwaipilot): agentic-coding post-training stack (AutoBuilder verifiable
  sandboxes, KwaiClawEnv, asymmetric PPO with a hindsight critic, MOPD).
  Hindsight-critic / harness-randomizer / data-flywheel interactives +
  3 real figures. Honest: PinchBench 94.9 (1st) but SWE-Bench Pro 65.2
  (2nd behind Opus 4.8), behind panel on Terminal-Bench; 16%->2% etc. are
  engineering deltas not ablations; benchmarks provider-run.
- musacoder-gpu-kernels (arXiv 2606.04847, Moore Threads): execution-
  feedback RL for GPU-kernel generation. Correctness-first reward wall,
  PrimeEcho anchor, Buffered Dynamic Retry, MirrorPop mean-abs masking.
  Reward-ladder / prime-echo / mirror-pop interactives + 2 real figures.
  Honest: benchmarks/verifier/baselines provider-run, speed is the weak
  axis (~15% faster rate), reward weights undisclosed (illustrative).

All to the diagram bar (bounded loops, no RNG/Date, theme-aware, no
font-family). pnpm validate green (51 articles).

subject: Add two edge-inference explainers: DGX Spark batching + Colibri
body:
- dgx-spark-batching: one DGX Spark serving many concurrent users on
  Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (MoE ~3B active, 4-bit NVFP4/FP8) via vLLM continuous
  batching. Continuous-batch scene (streams join/leave a fused forward
  pass, per-stream paged KV caches) + throughput curve pinned to committed
  spark_bench.csv points. Honest: aggregate vs per-user (~11 tok/s each),
  throughput != latency; 64-user / 700-tok/s / 54s / 38W are framed as
  reported, not verified (repo's real 723 tok/s is a different model at
  concurrency 32; no power column exists).
- colibri (JustVugg/colibri, Apache-2.0): pure-C engine running GLM-5.2
  744B MoE on ~25GB RAM by keeping the int4 dense core (~9.9GB) resident
  and streaming 21,504 experts (~370GB) from disk with LRU cache + async
  readahead + MTP. Three-tier memory/expert-streaming visualizer + a
  throughput ladder + memory-budget scene. Honest: feasibility feat not
  usable speed (0.05-0.37 tok/s), and '25GB RAM' needs ~370GB of disk.

pnpm validate green.

subject: Guard ScaleBank PRNG loops with an iteration cap
body:
Both routed() and ngramHit() sample during SSR/prerender. Cap the
sampling loops at MAX_SPIN iterations so a degenerate PRNG can never
hang next build again; the RNG converges in a few steps, so the cap
only trips on a bug.

subject: Fix infinite loop in LongCat ScaleBank ngramHit that hung the build
body:
ngramHit()'s PRNG used a single left shift (x ^ (x << 11)). Shifting
left by 11 zeroes the low 11 bits, so bits 0-3 of x are unchanged every
iteration; with COLS=16, x % COLS reads exactly those frozen bits and
returns the same value forever. The set never reaches size 2, so
while (set.size < 2) spins infinitely.

ScaleBank calls ngramHit() synchronously during render, so next build
hung at 'Generating static pages' (0/250) and timed out after 45 min on
Vercel. Add a right-shift mixing step (matching routed()) so the low
bits actually change; verified the loop terminates and the full build
now completes 250/250.

subject: Add LongCat 2.0 flagship explainer (1.6T open MoE + LongCat Sparse Attention)
body:
Meituan's LongCat 2.0: 1.6T-total / ~48B-active MoE, MIT-licensed with
weights on HF, headlined by LongCat Sparse Attention (LSA). Built to the
diagram bar with three interactive SVG scenes and honest, fully-sourced
benchmarks.

Mechanism (from repo README + HF model card; no arXiv tech report exists):
- LSA = streaming-aware indexing (SI) + cross-layer indexing (CLI, one
  shared index every 2 layers, MTP draft steps share the pass) +
  hierarchical indexing (HI, coarse block recall -> fine token select).
  Addresses DeepSeek DSA's Lightning Indexer bottleneck. Note: SGLang
  deployment drops HI.
- N-gram Embedding: 135B params in sparse dims orthogonal to the MoE (a
  token-n-gram lookup, not experts).

Interactives:
- lsa-index: segmented 'MSA block top-k <-> LSA hierarchical' toggle,
  cross-linked to the MiniMax MSA article.
- scale-bank: 1.6T expert pool (~3% lit/token) + the 135B N-gram bank as
  a separate orthogonal store.
- cross-layer: CLI 'index per layer <-> shared every 2 layers' (6->3
  passes).

Honesty: benchmarks are provider-reported and LongCat 2.0 tops NO single
benchmark against the full frontier set (Gemini 3.1 Pro / GPT-5.5 / Opus
4.6-4.8) — it beats Gemini 3.1 Pro & GPT-5.5 on SWE-bench Pro and beats
Opus 4.8 on IFEval, but trails the best closed model on most. '1M context'
is a training-data claim (no published long-context eval); ASIC training
figures are provider claims; FP8 is a separate artifact. All flagged in a
warn callout + prose.

Figure: official 6-panel benchmark chart, served locally (no repo
architecture diagram exists). pnpm validate green (32 articles).

subject: Diagram polish pass: align older articles to the new guidelines
body:
Audited every interactive component in the older articles against
DIAGRAM_GUIDE.md and the block-select exemplar. No rebuilds were
needed — the prior redesign holds. Six targeted fixes for the small
guideline gaps that remained:

- agents-a1/horizon-scaling: drop hardcoded font-family (use font-mono
  class per the guide); closed-triangle arrowhead -> open chevron.
- set-diffusion/spectrum + window-knob: decode-step token strips were a
  categorical rainbow; encode step as a single-accent lightness ramp
  (rule 4 - sequential, not a rainbow of hues).
- unconventional-un-0/phase-dials: per-dial hue was keyed to an
  arbitrary index; key it to each oscillator's phase instead, so the
  colors converge as coupling locks them - a meaningful encoding.
- coroutines-in-c/coroutine-stepper: two straight-line connectors ->
  gentle cubic-bezier arcs, consistent with the other edges.
- fastlio2/ikd-map: drop hardcoded font-family.
- fastlio2/{ikd-map,iekf-register}: fix a dead ternary so the Stat
  'highlight' prop actually changes weight.

Typecheck + content validate green (31 articles); overflow/layout-shift
sweep clean in light and dark at mobile and desktop.

subject: Add four flagship explainers: Cosmos 3, MrFlow, Nemotron NVFP4, TabFM
body:
Each ships distill-grade interactive SVG diagrams plus the real paper
figures (served locally), BenchBars, and honest caveats:

- cosmos-world-model: MoT two-tower (AR Reasoner + diffusion Generator)
  with shared dual-stream joint attention, Action-CoT, one-backbone.
  Honest: best-open not best-general (Gemini 3.1 Pro 77.5 > Super 73.7),
  dated leaderboard snapshots, provider-selected T2I harness, best-of-N.
- mrflow-diffusion-acceleration: training-free 4-stage staged sampling
  (LR generate -> pixel-space SR -> low-strength noise re-inject -> 1 HR
  refine). Honest: within-1% is best-case, 25x needs distillation,
  degradation is model/config-dependent.
- nemotron-nvfp4: native 4-bit (NVFP4) training - E2M1 element + FP8
  per-16 block scale, 3 stabilizers (2D block-quant, Hadamard on wgrad,
  stochastic rounding), mixed precision. Honest: not end-to-end FP4,
  loss-gap != downstream parity, two real divergences + rollback.
- tabular-foundation-model: in-context tabular FM (TabPFN x TabICL
  lineage), column Set-Transformer -> row CLS compression -> 24-block
  ICL, trained on synthetic SCM tables. Honest: ensemble-vs-ensemble
  comparison, non-commercial weights, 10-class / ~500-feature ceilings.

Diagrams pass the overflow/layout-shift sweep in light+dark at mobile
and desktop; fixed a full-width bar label overflow in the NVFP4 memory
calculator. Content + typecheck validate (31 articles).

subject: Add Program-as-Weights explainer (interactive compiler + cost crossover)
body:
Flagship article on PAW (arXiv 2607.02512): compile a natural-language fuzzy
function into a LoRA adapter for a frozen 0.6B interpreter. Two distill-style
interactives — a compile-once/run-locally playground (pick a spec, watch the
adapter fill in, run inputs through the interpreter) and a draggable
cost-crossover chart (API-per-call vs compile-once economics) — plus three
real figures from the paper (architecture, program library, FuzzyBench
coverage). Honest caveats: coupled compiler/interpreter, non-interpretable
programs, single-step functions, synthetic training data.

subject: Add Leanstral explainer: proving theorems as a code agent
body:
Flagship article on Mistral's Leanstral 1.5 (119B/6B-active MoE Lean 4
theorem-proving code agent). Three original interactive components — the
code-agent loop stepper, a draggable PutnamBench test-time-scaling curve
(44 to 587 solved as the token budget grows), and an adversarial SafeVerify
gate — paired with five real figures from Mistral's report/blog (the agent
loop and RL verifier diagrams, the test-time-scaling curve, and the
benchmark charts), all served locally. Honest framing on cost and the
provers that rank higher under different conditions.

subject: Fix layout shift in animated components + wide-figure zoom cut-off
body:
Animated interactives that swapped variable-length per-step text (What/Why
boxes, captions, token rows) were changing height each frame and shoving the
whole page up/down while reading. Stabilized every affected component so its
height is constant across all animation states:
- grid-stack pattern: render all states overlaid in one cell, fade to the
  active one, so the box always sizes to the tallest state (model-factory,
  three-stage, distill-network, decouple, iekf-register, lio-flow, spectrum,
  taper-direction, turboquant/pipeline, retrieval-heads, hybrid-attention,
  mtp-training, decode-stream, pipeline-streams, prefill-decode caption).
- reserve token borders (transparent when borderless) so flex-wrap line count
  never changes (cache-grow, prefill-decode token rows).
- min-height reservation where text is computed, not from a fixed array
  (unmasking, mtp loss readout).
Verified across all 17 animated articles: page scrollHeight now stable
(Δ=0) over multiple animation cycles at a 390px mobile viewport.

Zoomable: wide figures were overflowing the fullscreen overlay and getting
centre-clipped on phones (the box could grow to max-w-6xl, far wider than the
screen). Bound the overlay box to the viewport (max-w-full) so wide figures
fit to width instead of being cut off.

Seven papers. OrbitQuant (rotate-then-Lloyd-Max, data-free, for diffusion

subject: Fix article dates to 2026-07-03
Add Laguna explainer: the Model Factory as the real contribution
body:
A flagship walk through Poolside's Laguna M.1 (225.8B/23.4B) and XS.2
(33.4B/3B, Apache-2.0) agentic-coding MoE models (arXiv 2605.27605) — framed
around the report's actual thesis: the 'Model Factory', an industrialized
versioned loop of data/training/eval/inference (Titan, Blender, Atlas, Hive,
Code Execution) where every run is reproducible code and a win ships by flipping
a flag, which let them build XS.2 from scratch in five weeks. Covers the MoE
architecture (XS.2's 3:1 SWA:GA hybrid), agentic RL on verifiable tasks from
real git commits (CISPO), quantization (bad quant hurts agentic more), and
honest SWE-bench numbers (competitive-not-leading; baselines Poolside-selected
on leak-patched images).

Two SSR/zero-JS interactives: the Model Factory flywheel and the M.1-vs-XS.2
total/active MoE comparison. Cross-links to MoE, MiMo, and Agents-A1.

subject: Add Set Diffusion explainer: one knob from autoregression to diffusion
body:
A flagship walk through Set Diffusion (Arriola & Kuleshov, Cornell, arXiv
2607.01775, ICML 2026): factorize generation over flexible-position,
flexible-length token SETS instead of fixed blocks, recovering AR, block
diffusion, and order-agnostic diffusion as special cases. The set-causal
architecture keeps a KV cache that updates every step (unlike diffusion, and
unlike block diffusion's per-block cache) while retaining any-order/infilling.
Covers the position-offset window knob (w->1/L gives the tight AR ELBO), and
honest results (beats block diffusion on GSM8K accuracy + throughput + infilling
at 110M scale; AR still leads left-to-right accuracy).

Two SSR/zero-JS interactives: the AR/block/set/diffusion spectrum (decode-order
reveal) and the window-w knob interpolating quality vs parallelism. Cross-links
to iLLaDA, TwoTower, and the inference piece.

subject: Add MiMo-V2-Flash explainer: hybrid sliding-window + global attention
body:
A flagship walk through Xiaomi MiMo-V2-Flash (arXiv 2601.02780): a 309B/15B-active
MoE (256 experts, top-8) that's the strongest open-source software-engineering
model. The focus is the hybrid attention — 8 blocks of 5 sliding-window (128-token)
layers + 1 global layer (5:1), GQA not MLA, learnable attention sink — which cuts
the KV cache ~6x while long-context quality holds. Covers MTP self-speculative
decoding (2.6x), the 27T-token FP8 training, the MOPD post-train, and honest
benchmarks (SWE-Bench Multilingual #1, LiveCodeBench/LongBench best-open, weaker on
HMMT/MRCR/HLE; 150 tok/s + pricing flagged as product-page marketing).

Two SSR/zero-JS interactives: the 5:1 hybrid block with compounding receptive
field, and the SWA KV-cache-growth chart. Cross-links to HydraHead, MTP, MoE,
MOPD/Agents-A1, and the inference piece.

subject: Add TurboVec explainer: FAISS-competitive vector search, no training phase
body:
A flagship walk through TurboVec (RyanCodrai/turbovec): a Rust vector index
with Python bindings implementing the TurboQuant algorithm — data-free rotation
+ Lloyd-Max scalar quantization, so there is no PQ-style training phase. ~16x
compression (10M vectors 31GB->4GB), matches/beats FAISS IndexPQ recall, and
runs 12-19% faster on ARM. Covers the six-step encode pipeline, the SIMD/systems
half (NEON/AVX-512, 32-vec blocks, O(1) removal, online ingest), and honest
scope (flat scan, not billion-scale HNSW).

Two SSR/zero-JS interactives: a corpus-in-RAM memory calculator and a
turbovec-vs-FAISS config explorer with the real benchmark numbers (including the
GloVe-2bit near-tie). Cross-links to the TurboQuant and BM25 pieces.

subject: Add TurboQuant explainer: rotate first, then quantize the KV cache
body:
A flagship walk through TurboQuant (Zandieh, Daliri, Hadian, Mirrokni / Google
Research, arXiv 2504.19874): a data-free, near-optimal vector quantizer that
rotates vectors into a known Beta distribution so one precomputed Lloyd-Max
quantizer fits every vector, with a 1-bit QJL residual to keep inner-product
estimates unbiased. Covers the KV-cache application (0xsero/turboquant, vLLM),
the asymmetric 3-bit-key/2-4-bit-value split, and the same primitive applied to
llama.cpp weights (turbo-tan TQ3, with the FP4/Blackwell prompt-processing
speedups) and vector search (turbovec).

Three SSR/zero-JS interactives: a live rotate-then-quantize demo (real
Walsh-Hadamard transform + quantizer, computed in-browser), the pipeline
step-through, and KV-cache memory vs context length. Honest flags on the
memory-not-throughput win, 2-bit value degradation, and MoE limitations.

subject: Add BM25 explainer: the ranking function that refuses to die
body:
A flagship explainer on Okapi BM25 built from first principles: TF-IDF and its
two flaws, fixed by term-frequency saturation (k1) and document-length
normalization (b). Walks the full Lucene formula term by term (non-negative IDF,
defaults k1=1.2/b=0.75), a ~30-line runnable Python implementation, the variant
family (BM25+, BM25F, BM25L), and why it endures as a baseline and the sparse
half of hybrid search.

Three SSR/zero-JS interactives: the TF-saturation curve (k1 slider), document-
length normalization (b slider), and a live client-side BM25 engine that scores
a 6-doc corpus against an editable query with per-term IDF x tf-saturation
breakdowns (verified numerically correct).

subject: Add TwoTower explainer: a diffusion LM with a frozen AR memory
body:
A flagship walk through Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower (NVIDIA, arXiv 2606.26493): a
block-wise autoregressive diffusion LM that decouples the two conflicting jobs
existing diffusion LMs share in one network — causal context representation and
bidirectional block denoising — into a frozen AR context tower and a trainable
denoiser tower that cross-attends to it. Built on Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B
(hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE); retains 98.7% of AR quality at 2.42x throughput.
Covers the architecture, the Mamba-chunk-equals-block trick, block-wise AR
diffusion with confidence sampling, and the tower-decoupling ablation.

Three SSR/zero-JS interactives: the two-tower architecture, block-wise AR
diffusion (causal across blocks, parallel within), and the decoupling ablation.
Benchmark + throughput bars from the paper's tables; honest flags on the
figure-only category comparison, the absence of dLLM baselines, and the
code/math quality gap. Cross-links to iLLaDA, inference, and HydraHead.

subject: Add Tapered Language Models explainer
body:
A flagship walk through Tapered Language Models (Bayat, Behrouz, Courville,
arXiv 2606.23670): under a fixed parameter budget, taper the MLP hidden width
across depth with a cosine schedule (1.5x early to 0.5x late), improving
perplexity and downstream accuracy at no extra params or FLOPs — while the
reverse allocation hurts. Covers the residual-stream evidence (later layers
refine, not transform), the exact cosine schedule, the direction/shape/strength
ablations, results across four architectures and three scales, and the
residual-alignment mechanism.

Three SSR/zero-JS interactives: the taper schedule (uniform vs tapered at fixed
budget), the where-the-work-happens evidence chart, and the taper-direction
ablation (front-load wins, back-load hurts). Benchmark bars + a results table
from the paper; cross-links to the MoE and HydraHead pieces.

subject: Add HydraHead explainer: hybrid attention at the head, not the layer
body:
A flagship walk through HydraHead (Alibaba, arXiv 2606.20097): hybridizing
full and linear (Gated DeltaNet) attention along the HEAD axis instead of the
usual per-layer blocks. Covers the interpretability finding (layers vary
smoothly, heads within a layer specialize — only ~6.5% of heads are retrieval-
critical), causal path-patching head selection, scale-normalized fusion for
the FA/LA distribution gap, and the three-stage parameter-reuse + distillation
transfer. Honest flags on the Figure-1-only 512K/Qwen3.5 claims.

Three SSR/zero-JS interactives: attention-cost-vs-context (n^2 vs n slider),
head specialization / retrieval-head viz, and the head-wise vs layer-wise
hybridization grid. Benchmark bars from the paper's RULER tables; cross-links
to the attention, inference, and GLM-5 pieces.

subject: arXiv digest 2026-07-01: on-policy distillation + long-horizon agents
body:
Seven papers curated. Threads: on-policy distillation compounding (GR2's OPD
over collapsing SFT, Cross-Space Distillation, browser skill distillation) and
rigorous evaluation of dense supervision for long-horizon agents (QVal). Plus
PointSplat and FLORA on the vision/LiDAR side.

subject: Add Agents-A1 explainer: scaling the agent horizon, not the parameters
body:
A flagship walk through InternScience's Agents-A1 — a 35B MoE (~3B active,
Qwen3.5-35B-A3B base) that reaches the trillion-parameter agentic-benchmark
band by scaling the horizon instead of the parameter count. Covers the two
axes (45K-token long-horizon trajectories + six heterogeneous domains), the
knowledge-action graph data engine (proposer-solver-verifier self-play), and
the three-stage recipe (full-domain SFT, domain teachers, multi-teacher
domain-routed on-policy distillation with salient vocabulary alignment).

Four SSR/zero-JS interactives: horizon-vs-parameters thesis scatter (real
numbers), a long-horizon agent loop, the three-stage pipeline, and the
multi-teacher distillation router. Benchmark bars from the tech report; cross-
links to today's on-policy-distillation arXiv digest and the inference piece.

subject: FAST-LIO2: fix the real-bag drift; embed the full single-file Python
body:
The minimal filter tracked the Avia bag but the map drifted — doubled,
smeared walls and a late runaway. Two real bugs, both now fixed in
fastlio2_mini.py:

- Stub deskew. It dropped each point into the nearest IMU pose with no
  within-sweep motion compensation, so every fast-motion scan was warped.
  Now it interpolates the IMU-propagated trajectory (SO(3) for rotation,
  linear for position) to each point's capture time. Per-plane map
  thickness drops to ~4 cm; the doubled walls collapse to one.
- Over-tight association gate. A range-normalized point-to-plane gate copied
  verbatim from FAST-LIO starved the sparse, voxel-downsampled scans of all
  correspondences when the prediction drifted, so the update was skipped and
  the pose dead-reckoned away. A gentler metric gate keeps hundreds of
  inliers per scan and the filter stays locked.

Result on the full HKU Avia bag (491 scans / 49 s): returns to within ~0.6 m
of start over 38 m of path (~1.6% drift), crisp single-walled room, no
divergence. New trajectory+map figure; the real-data section is rewritten
as a three-bug arc (clock mismatch, deskew, gate). Also embeds the whole
~390-line file inline via a collapsible CodeCollapse block, and fixes a
hydration mismatch in that component.

subject: Fix image expand overlay (portal), add collapsible code block, arXiv 2026-06-30
body:
- Zoomable: render the fullscreen overlay through a portal to document.body so
  it escapes any transformed/filtered ancestor that was making fixed-position
  break (the overlay was rendering off-screen for figures far down the page,
  showing blank). Also make the expand button visible on touch (no hover).
- New CodeCollapse mdx component: shows a long code block at a preview height
  with a 'show full file' expander; degrades to the full listing with no JS.
- arXiv digest for 2026-06-30 (3D Gaussian SLAM + on-policy distillation).

subject: FAST-LIO2: minimal Python now tracks the real Avia bag
body:
The offline filter collapsed on the HKU Livox Avia bag because the Livox
CustomMsg header is stamped on the sensor's boot clock while the IMU uses
the bag record clock — so the propagate-to-scan-end loop never fired and
the IMU never advanced the state. Use the bag record time for every scan's
t_end, ignore the Livox header. With that fixed, plus the avia.yaml
LiDAR->IMU extrinsic, calibrated IMU noise, and per-scan voxel downsampling,
fastlio2_mini tracks the full bag (491 scans / 49 s, ~47 m path) and
reconstructs the room. Rewrites the real-data section around the clock-sync
bug and adds a trajectory+map figure.

subject: Add KV-cache-growth diagram; honest FAST-LIO2 real-data calibration note
body:
- llm-inference: a new animated "KV cache growth" interactive — prefill writes N
  entries in one pass, each decode step appends one (bright) and reuses the rest
  (faded), with running entry counts and outputs. Makes the linear-growth
  mechanic concrete before the linear-vs-quadratic cost chart.
- fast-lio2: record the calibrated real-bag attempt honestly. Adding the Avia
  extrinsic, the real avia.yaml IMU covariances (acc/gyr_cov = 0.1), and
  gravity-aligned init still collapses — and explain precisely why: an
  over-confident update drives velocity to zero into a self-consistent
  "stationary" fixed-point, compounded by the Avia's narrow-FOV non-repetitive
  pattern making 5-NN plane associations degenerate. That bootstrap/association
  gap, not the equations, is what FAST-LIO2's full machinery solves.

subject: Drop committed __pycache__, add to .gitignore
FAST-LIO2: add full runnable Python (offline, .bag), flow, per-step explanations
body:
Make the FAST-LIO2 explainer reproducible end to end:

- public/articles/fastlio2/fastlio2_mini.py — a complete, dependency-light
  (~250 line) implementation: SO(3), the on-manifold ESEKF (forward propagation
  + iterated point-to-plane update with the reformulated gain), backward-
  propagation deskew, a cKDTree map, IMU init, and an offline driver. Validated
  on a synthetic room to ~4 cm ATE, and via a real ROS1 .bag round-trip to ~6 cm.
- read_bag() reads ROS1 bags with the pure-python `rosbags` (no ROS install) and
  handles BOTH sensor_msgs/PointCloud2 and Livox livox_ros_driver/CustomMsg
  (registers the custom type and parses per-point offset_time for deskew).
- Article: an animated step-by-step flow interactive (what each stage does and
  why), the full driver code, the synthetic + .bag demo numbers, the gdown +
  Livox-parsing recipe, and an honest account of running it on the real HKU Avia
  bag — it reads and runs all 491 scans but the minimal filter covariance-collapses
  on 0.9 rad/s handheld motion, which is the lesson on what production FAST-LIO2's
  in-state gravity, calibrated extrinsic, ikd-Tree, and covariance handling buy.

subject: Add FAST-LIO2 explainer: LiDAR-inertial odometry you can reproduce
body:
A distill-standard walk through FAST-LIO2 (arXiv 2107.06829) that teaches the
mechanism and a no-ROS reproduction path. Covers the on-manifold error-state, IMU
forward propagation, backward-propagation deskew, the direct point-to-plane
measurement, the iterated EKF update with its reformulated (information-form)
Kalman gain — K = (HᵀR⁻¹H + P⁻¹)⁻¹HᵀR⁻¹, which makes complexity depend on state
dimension not the thousands of point measurements — and the ikd-Tree incremental
map. C++ snippets are sourced from the clean zlwang7/S-FAST_LIO reimplementation
(explicit ESEKF, not the IKFoM template layer), with simplified runnable NumPy
alongside, a what's-ROS-vs-core table, a file→concept map, and honest limits.

Three original interactives: scan deskewing (motion compensation), the iterated
point-to-plane registration converging onto map planes, and the ikd-Tree moving
map window. Real system/measurement/map/runtime figures from the paper.

subject: Add Kalman filter explainer
body:
A first-principles walk through the Kalman filter as Gaussian fusion: the
predict-update cycle, the gain as the optimal trust ratio, and the full matrix
equations, with runnable NumPy. Builds up to the EKF, the iterated EKF, and the
on-manifold error-state filter — the bridge to LiDAR-inertial SLAM.

Two original interactives: a live constant-velocity tracker with an uncertainty
band and R/Q trust sliders, and a prediction × measurement = posterior Gaussian
fusion showing the gain and the always-sharper posterior. Plus a predict-update
loop SVG and a Joseph-form numerical-stability note.

subject: Add MegaTrain explainer: training a 120B model on one GPU
body:
A systems walk through MegaTrain (arXiv 2604.05091): a memory-centric runtime
that keeps every parameter, gradient, and Adam moment in host RAM and treats the
GPU as a transient compute engine streaming one layer at a time — training up to
120B params at full precision on a single H200. Covers the 12-bytes/param memory
inversion, the three-phase step (streaming forward, block-wise backward, CPU
Adam), the double-buffered three-CUDA-stream pipeline that hides PCIe (the -31.3%
ablation), and the supporting tricks (stateless layer templates, layer-contiguous
tiling, pinned slab pool, block-wise recompute). Honest on scope: single-GPU only,
TFLOPS-not-MFU, regime-specific speedups near the memory cliff, hardware-dependent.

Two original interactives — a host-vs-GPU memory-placement explorer showing where
each system OOMs, and an animated double-buffered CUDA-stream pipeline. Real
architecture/timeline/scaling figures and benchmark tables from the paper.

subject: Fix interactive UI: blank KV-cache bars and layout shift on streaming
body:
- kv-cache: the work-per-step bars were blank — bar height was a % against a
  zero-height (auto) parent, so it collapsed to 0. Give each column h-full so the
  percentage resolves, and always colour the bars by mode (flat green with cache,
  growing coral staircase without) instead of gating them on an animation pointer
  that flickered blank on each loop.
- Stop the auto-playing components from shifting the whole page as generated text
  wraps to new lines: reserve the final layout up front.
  - decode-stream: invisible full-text spacer fixes the height; the streamed
    prefix overlays it.
  - prefill-decode: render all output tokens (future ones invisible) and all KV
    slots (inactive ones faint) so neither row grows.
  - illada unmasking: every word keeps its full width whether masked (transparent
    text in a dashed box) or revealed, so rows never reflow.
  - moa-roundtable: proposer and aggregator text are always present and revealed
    via opacity, reserving their height.

subject: Add LLM inference explainer: prefill, decode, and the KV cache
body:
A first-principles walk through how a generate() call actually runs: the
compute-bound prefill phase (TTFT) versus the memory-bound decode phase (ITL),
the full path from BPE tokens to streamed output, and why the KV cache dominates
serving economics. Covers cache mitigations (quantization, sliding window, GQA,
PagedAttention), attention redesigned around the cache (DeepSeek-V4 CSA/HCA), the
quantization memory ladder, and the serving layer (continuous batching,
speculative decoding), closing with how to diagnose prefill- vs decode-bound.

Three original interactives — an animated prefill->decode visualizer with the
compute/memory bottleneck flip, a with/without KV-cache work comparison plus a
cache-memory slider, and a precision/size to footprint to fits-on-GPU explorer.
Cross-links the iLLaDA, DSpark, and MTP articles.

subject: Add iLLaDA explainer: scaling a masked-diffusion language model
body:
A walk through iLLaDA (arXiv 2606.25331) — an 8B fully-bidirectional masked
diffusion LM trained from scratch on 12T tokens, successor to LLaDA. Covers the
masked-diffusion mechanism (forward masking, the 1/t-weighted NELBO objective,
bidirectional attention, iterative-unmasking generation), what iLLaDA changes
(GQA, 12T-token scale-up, variable-length generation, confidence-based scoring),
and the honest result: base-model parity with Qwen2.5 but a ~10-point instruct
gap, with efficiency asserted rather than measured.

Three original interactives plus custom train/generate and SFT-ablation SVG
diagrams; benchmark tables and BenchBars from the paper's Tables 1-3.

subject: Add arXiv digest for 2026-06-28

subject: Add four AI explainers + today's arXiv digest
body:
Four research-grounded, dual-native explainer articles, each with interactive

subject: Build ai.thesatyajit.com: AI-native dual-native personal site
body:
A Next.js 16 personal site where every page is both a human document and an
agent-readable artifact, maintained by a crew of Claude agents.

Site:
- Content layer (MDX + gray-matter + Zod 4): blog, articles, logs, projects,
  arxiv digest, snippets, notes — malformed frontmatter fails the build.
- Pages: home (ask-my-site console), about, resume (HTML + PDF synced from the
  resume repo + JSON Resume), 10 projects with screenshots, /health Biomap,
  github stats (dummy fallback), now/uses/reading, publications, patents,
  colophon, changelog, search.
- Interactive MDX kit: math, diagrams, step-through, attention matrix, an
  animated coroutine stepper, plots.
- Editorial x terminal design: Hanken Grotesk + IBM Plex Mono, sticky header
  with command palette, system dark mode, Phosphor icons, custom 404.

Agent layer:
- llms.txt / llms-full.txt / ai.txt, per-page .md variants, JSON-LD, JSON API,
  feed.xml, openapi.json, sitemap, AI-welcoming robots.
- Hosted MCP server (mcp-handler) + ask_satyajit RAG, both via the Vercel AI SDK
  (Gemini), degrading gracefully with no key.
- Per-route OG images, share buttons, the md/json/mcp chip on every page.

brand-crew plugin: skills, slash commands, agents, validate + session hooks that
author content and ship via PR; social drafted, never auto-posted.

All env vars optional; site builds and runs with none.
